high.spark: A reinforcement learning system for automated mathematics solved the exact problem your anchor mechanism is trying to solve — how to guide a search without predetermining where it lands — by splitting the scoring layer completely away from the search path.
high.source_shape: FERMAT is an RL environment that forms mathematical theories by evolving an 'interestingness' function: a learned measure that scores candidate outputs as worth keeping or not, without specifying which outputs to generate. The search roams combinatorially vast spaces of mathematical objects freely; the interestingness function only fires after a candidate surfaces, judging it without having directed the path to it. Critically, the interestingness function itself is never fully defined in advance — it is synthesized and sharpened through the process, gaining definition from what it encounters.
high.bridge: Your anchor plays the same structural role as FERMAT's interestingness function: it does not tell the wandering agents where to go, it recognizes whether what they found is worth surfacing. The split FERMAT makes explicit — search mechanism on one side, scoring mechanism on the other — is the same split your architecture depends on when it lets agents wander freely and then measures resonance against the anchor after the fact. There is a second correspondence: FERMAT's interestingness function starts incomplete and sharpens through contact with the search results, which mirrors the way a user's anchor is a partial skeleton that gains definition through what it successfully recognizes. The anchor does not need to be fully articulated in advance; like FERMAT's scoring function, it becomes more itself through the process of matching.
high.use: Consider examining how FERMAT's LLM-based evolutionary algorithm handles abstraction across structurally similar but surface-different mathematical objects — that mechanism is the closest published analog to cross-domain resonance scoring, and studying how it avoids requiring symbolic grounding might surface a concrete design move for your own scoring layer.
high.limit: FERMAT operates entirely inside a single formally bounded domain — elementary number theory and finite fields — where every object has a crisp symbolic definition and every claim is verifiably true or false. That formal verifiability is what makes the RL reward signal tractable. Constellax's wandering is explicitly cross-domain and metaphorical: whether a theater concept resonates with a physics paper is irreducibly interpretive, not computable. The bridge holds for the structural shape — scoring without directing, incompleteness that sharpens through use — but breaks at the question of how to operationalize that scoring function when there is no formal ground truth to close the loop on.

high.spark: A reinforcement learning system for automated mathematics had to solve the exact same tension your system does: deterministic search kills surprise, pure randomness kills usefulness — so it learned a soft scoring function that guides exploration without ever specifying the destination.
high.source_shape: Fermat frames mathematical theory formation as a Markov Decision Process: an agent traverses a space of mathematical concepts and operations, building new theorems step by step, with no long-range memory of where it has been. The problem it had to solve is that neither a fixed search strategy nor random mutation produces interesting mathematics — so the system learns an 'interestingness function' (IF) through an evolutionary algorithm, a scoring mechanism that recognizes when a newly formed concept is worth pursuing without ever being told in advance what 'worth pursuing' means. The IF is the middle path: rich enough to recognize signal when the wander encounters it, loose enough to permit traversal into regions that were never named before training.
high.bridge: Your anchor plays the role of the interestingness function. It does not tell the wandering agents where to go — it scores what they bring back, pulling toward resonance without predetermining the path. The wandering agents' traversal maps onto Fermat's MDP: each hop is a local move made without long-range memory of the full trajectory, and the only coherent map of where the agent went is the retrospective path it leaves behind. The structural match goes one level deeper: Fermat's core causal claim — that determinism collapses novelty and pure randomness collapses utility — is the same causal primitive your mechanism layer names as the reason a soft anchor is necessary at all. The IF is not just analogous to your anchor; it is an implemented, tested instance of the same mechanism operating in a different substrate.
high.use: Consider examining Fermat's evolutionary algorithm for the IF as a concrete existence proof that this kind of scoring function can be learned rather than hand-specified — if your anchor's scoring logic ever needs to be made more explicit or adaptive, the IF architecture is a worked example of how that problem was solved in a formally tractable domain.
high.limit: Fermat's interestingness function is trained and evaluated entirely within a single formally bounded domain — elementary number theory and Galois fields. It learns what is interesting inside mathematics, and it would need to be rebuilt from scratch for any other domain. Your system's wandering is explicitly cross-domain and unconstrained: the anchor must recognize structural resonance across theater, physics, literature, and mathematics simultaneously, using the same scoring logic. The IF has no mechanism for cross-domain structural transfer, which is precisely the three-point triangulation your architecture depends on — so the bridge holds tightly for the freedom-within-constraint shape and the soft-scoring mechanism, but breaks completely at the question of how a single anchor function generalizes across domains it was never trained on.

# Learning Interestingness in Automated Mathematic

high.spark: Mathematical invention works by merging two incomplete theories — and it is precisely their incompleteness that makes the merge productive. Your system is built on the same principle: the half-formed concept is not a problem to fix before the work begins, it is the mechanism by which the work begins.
high.source_shape: Bou et al. model mathematical discovery as conceptual blending: two partial theories — say, a fragment of natural number arithmetic and a fragment of functional analysis — are fed into a formal process that finds their shared skeleton (the 'generic space') and constructs a new theory, called the colimit, that inherits structure from both without being identical to either. The key move is that neither input theory is complete. Full, closed theories have no room left for emergent structure — every consequence is already derivable. It is the gaps, the missing axioms, the unresolved tensions between the two inputs, that the blending process fills with something genuinely new. The system then 'runs' the blend: it derives theorems that were not present in either input, discovers inconsistencies, weakens axioms to resolve them, and iterates.
high.bridge: The anchor's soft-anchor mechanism and Bou et al.'s generic space are the same structural object: something rich enough to recognize that two things belong together, but empty enough not to predetermine what emerges from that recognition. In both cases, the anchor/generic-space does not say 'go here' — it says 'these two things are in structural conversation, now run the blend and see what falls out.' The paper's operationalization of incompleteness-as-match-surface maps directly onto the anchor's treatment of the user's half-formed concept: a fully articulated, closed concept would be like a fully axiomatized theory — nothing new can emerge because there is no open surface for incoming structure to attach to. The user's vagueness is not noise to be cleaned before the system runs; it is the exact feature that makes resonance possible. Where the paper uses iterative inconsistency-resolution to crystallize the blend into a stable new theory, your system's process of surfacing connections and letting the user respond is doing the same work — the card the user engages with is the blend being 'run,' and the user's reaction is the inconsistency-resolution step that tightens or weakens the emerging structure.
high.use: Consider framing the user's concept not as an input to be clarified before the system works, but as a deliberately partial axiomatization — and design the card-response loop explicitly as a blending iteration: each card the user engages with adds a constraint that tightens the generic space, and each card they reject weakens an implicit axiom, until the colimit (their advanced concept) crystallizes from the accumulated resolutions.
high.limit: The blending process in Bou et al. is fully deterministic and formally verifiable: given the two input spaces and the morphisms connecting them to the generic space, the colimit is uniquely computed and its consistency is checkable by machine. That requires the human to name both input theories and identify the generic space before the machinery runs — the entire structure is drawn in advance, then executed. Your system's premise is the opposite: the user cannot and should not name those spaces, because the wandering agents are what surface them. There is also no counterpart in the paper to the retrospective-path-as-coherent-map: in conceptual blending, the diagram precedes the blend; in your system, the only coherent account of why a path was productive emerges after the path is walked. The bridge holds tightly for the incompleteness-as-productive-surface and generic-space-as-soft-anchor shapes, but breaks entirely at the question of who specifies the morphisms and when.

# The rol

high.spark: A benchmark that forces AI agents to infer physical laws from a tight measurement budget — and breaks agents that rely on memorized knowledge — turns out to be a working model of how productive incompleteness actually functions: partial signal isn't noise to be resolved, it's the structural skeleton that guides the search.
high.source_shape: MaD Physics presents agents with a physical system governed by a law that may have been altered — spring constants shifted, pendulum parameters changed — and gives them a limited number of observations before demanding a prediction. Agents that try to exhaustively measure everything run out of budget before they can act; agents that move randomly waste measurements on uninformative states. The agents that succeed are the ones that treat each partial observation as a structural clue — something that narrows the space of possible laws without yet resolving it — and use that narrowing to decide where to look next. Crucially, when the underlying law is altered, agents trained on the standard version fail badly, because their memorized priors override the signal the environment is actually emitting.
high.bridge: Your system's wandering agents face the same three-way tension: exhaustive retrieval collapses novelty (the agent finds what is already known), pure drift collapses utility (the agent finds what is merely distant), and the anchor is what makes the middle path possible. In MaD Physics, the measurement budget is the analog of your agents' finite traversal — it forces the agent to act on partial information rather than wait for certainty. The altered physical law is the analog of your user's anchor: a structural shape that is real and recoverable, but not yet explicit, and not matchable by memorized pattern. And the agent's behavior of treating partial observations as a skeleton — not noise, not a complete picture, but a genuine partial structure that constrains the next move — is exactly what your 'incompleteness as maximum match surface' mechanism claims: the anchor's vagueness is not a deficiency to be resolved before the search begins, it is what keeps the search space open enough to find resonance the user didn't already know to name.
high.use: Consider testing whether your agents perform differently when they are explicitly rewarded for returning findings that partially match the anchor rather than findings that fully satisfy it — MaD Physics suggests that the agents trained to act on incomplete structural signal outperform those waiting for resolution, and you could design a scoring pass that privileges 'structurally adjacent but not obvious' returns over high-confidence direct matches.
high.limit: MaD Physics operates inside a single, closed environment per trial — one physical system, one law, a known structural form even when its parameters are altered. Your agents wander across unconstrained domains simultaneously: theater, mathematics, biology, whatever the traversal reaches. The benchmark's 'altered law' is still a law — it has a recoverable symbolic form, and the agent's job is parameter inference inside a known family. Your anchor is user-generated and deliberately vague, with no recoverable symbolic form and no guarantee that the underlying structure belongs to any named family at all. The bridge holds for the shape of acting-on-partial-signal, but breaks at the question of how an agent knows when it has found a genuine structural match versus a superficial one — MaD Physics has ground truth; your system does not.

## MaD Physics

 Evaluating Information Seeking Under Constraints in Physical Environments

, Mehdi Bennani 1, Johanne

high.spark: A branch of mathematics built to explain how new concepts are born treats incompleteness not as a flaw to fix but as the very surface that makes merging possible — which is exactly the structural bet your system is making.
high.source_shape: Conceptual Blending theory, formalized here using a construction called a colimit, describes how a genuinely new concept emerges when two partial structures from different domains are merged into a blended space that inherits properties from both inputs but cannot be reduced to either one alone. The colimit acts as a minimal common scaffold: it does not dictate what the blend will look like, it only identifies the structural relationships that must be preserved for the merge to be coherent. Critically, when two input spaces are inconsistent with each other, the formalism does not discard the blend — it weakens the conflicting constraints just enough to restore consistency, treating the tension as something to negotiate rather than a reason to stop.
high.bridge: Your system's anchor plays the same role as the generic space in blending theory: it is not a target the agent must reach, it is a relational skeleton that tells the agent which finds are structurally compatible with the user's concept. The colimit's 'score without directing' property is the formal version of your soft anchor — it provides just enough constraint to recognize a coherent merge without predetermining what gets merged. The paper's treatment of incompleteness as generative maps directly onto your system's assumption that a vague, unfinished anchor is an asset rather than a liability: the less the anchor specifies, the more structural surface it exposes for an unexpected find to attach to. And the weakening move — negotiating inconsistency rather than rejecting it — suggests that when an agent surfaces something that only partially fits the anchor, the right response is not discard but partial inheritance, letting the user's concept absorb what fits and leave the rest.
high.use: Consider treating partial or tension-producing matches not as low-confidence noise to filter out, but as candidates for a weakening pass — a step that asks 'what is the minimal adjustment to the anchor's framing that would make this find coherent?' and surfaces that adjusted framing to the user alongside the find itself.
high.limit: The formalism here is entirely human-driven: a mathematician consciously selects both input spaces and the generic space that mediates them before the colimit is ever computed. The theory is a precise account of how to blend once you already know what to blend — it has no mechanism for autonomous traversal, and no way to score an unknown find against a vague anchor before the blend is even attempted. Your agents must solve the upstream problem the paper assumes away: discovering candidate input spaces in the first place, without a human pre-selecting them. The bridge holds for the blending and incompleteness-handling shape; it breaks entirely at the question of how to wander.

# Conceptual Blending in Mathematical Cre

high.spark: A branch of AI formalized how to build genuinely new concepts by finding the structural skeleton two theories share — not retrieving what's similar, but synthesizing what neither contained alone. That skeleton logic is the same mechanism your anchor uses to recognize resonance without predetermining it.
high.source_shape: The paper formalizes 'theory blending': given two input theories L and R, an algorithm finds their least-general common structure — called the generalization space G — using a technique called anti-unification. G is not a summary of either theory; it is the minimal relational skeleton that both theories satisfy. Candidate blends are then scored by how well they preserve the topology of G while introducing new content neither L nor R contained. The key move is that G acts as a recognition surface — rich enough to identify when a blend is structurally coherent, but loose enough to leave the actual content of the blend undetermined.
high.bridge: Your anchor plays the role of G. When a user gives Constellax their half-formed pursuit and unresolved inspirations, that input is not a query to be matched against a database — it is a structural skeleton that defines what counts as resonant without specifying what the resonant thing will look like. The wandering agent's job is anti-unification in reverse: traverse a domain, find structures that share relational topology with the anchor, and surface them as candidate blends. The paper's scoring logic — does this candidate preserve the skeleton's topology while adding something neither input contained? — is exactly the test a well-functioning Constellax card should pass. And the paper's central finding, that the generalization space enables synthesis rather than retrieval, names why the anchor must stay loose: a tighter anchor would retrieve known things; a skeletal anchor creates the conditions for something new to emerge.
high.use: Consider auditing your anchor's representation against the paper's generalization space concept — specifically, ask whether the anchor is currently storing enough relational structure (how the user's concepts connect to each other) rather than just topical content, since it is relational topology, not subject matter, that enables cross-domain resonance of the kind this paper formalizes.
high.limit: This formalization requires both input theories to be fully axiomatized in many-sorted first-order logic before the algorithm can run — the inputs must be logically coherent and complete. Constellax's anchor is the opposite: deliberately pre-axiomatic, structurally incomplete, and valuable precisely because of that incompleteness. The paper's framework would treat the anchor's vagueness as a preprocessing deficiency to resolve before blending can begin; Constellax treats that same vagueness as the maximum match surface — the thing that keeps the recognition space open. The two systems are structurally analogous up to that point, and diverge exactly where the user's incompleteness matters most.

high.spark: An AI that must infer the laws of physics from a handful of expensive measurements — never allowed to just observe everything — is solving the same structural problem as a system that must find what's most relevant to a concept without being told what to look for.
high.source_shape: MaD Physics is a benchmark where an agent studies a physical system operating under deliberately altered laws — laws the agent cannot have memorized — and must figure out those laws using only a strict budget of measurements. Each measurement costs something, so the agent cannot simply observe until certainty arrives; it must choose which observations will unlock the most inference from the least data. The design forces the agent to work from a partial skeleton of evidence and reason forward, rather than pattern-match against prior knowledge.
high.bridge: Your system's anchor works the same way a MaD Physics budget works: it doesn't tell the wandering agent where to go, it just makes some directions more expensive than others by scoring resonance softly rather than directing the path. The agent's partial traversal of a domain — never complete, always constrained — is structurally identical to the MaD Physics agent's sparse measurement set: both are incomplete by design, and that incompleteness is what keeps the possibility space open long enough to find something genuinely surprising. The 'altered laws' design choice maps onto your inheritance-vs-transcendence tension: MaD Physics deliberately breaks known physics so the agent cannot retrieve a memorized answer, which is exactly the condition your wandering agents need to satisfy — the find must be a genuine structural resonance, not a cached association. And just as a MaD Physics agent that over-commits to early measurements collapses the inference space prematurely, a wandering agent that locks onto the anchor's most obvious surface meaning will stop finding the non-obvious bridges.
high.use: Consider whether your wandering agents have an explicit 'over-specification brake' — a signal that fires when the agent's path is converging too tightly on the anchor's literal terms too early, analogous to the MaD Physics budget that forces the agent to stay in the inferential space longer before committing.
high.limit: MaD Physics is a convergent system with a finish line: the agent's incompleteness is a temporary constraint it is actively trying to eliminate, and success means closing the gap to a single correct answer. Your system treats vagueness as a permanent structural feature to be preserved, not a problem to be solved — the anchor is never meant to be fully resolved into a definitive match. MaD Physics also operates entirely within a single domain with a known target structure; your wandering agents cross unconstrained domain space looking for structural resonance in theater, biology, or wherever the wander leads, and MaD Physics has no mechanism that corresponds to that cross-domain traversal at all.

high.source_shape: Fuzzy Logic with a Gagné Learning Hierarchy (FL-GLH) takes imprecise linguistic inputs — 'Poor,' 'Developing,' 'Good' — and runs them through a fuzzifier that preserves the vagueness structurally rather than converting it to a precise number. Underneath this sits a Gagné hierarchy: a prerequisite skill chain where each level depends on the one below it, so a gap at any point is a precise locator of where learning broke down. The system only collapses vagueness into a crisp output at the very last step — defuzzification — meaning the entire diagnostic process operates on soft, overlapping categories rather than sharp distinctions.
high.bridge: Your anchor mechanism — a soft score that guides without directing — is exactly what the fuzzy membership function implements: a 59 and a 60 are not categorically different, so the system doesn't force a hard boundary that would eliminate valid matches near the edge. This maps directly to your claim that full articulation narrows the match surface to near-zero: the moment you resolve vagueness prematurely, you lose the productive overlap zone where resonance lives. The Gagné hierarchy maps to the structural skeleton node — it is a minimum viable diagnostic scaffold, not a complete map, and partial knowledge at one level is treated as a real signal about barrier location rather than noise. The four grouped inputs FL-GLH uses as its diagnostic frame mirror your three-point triangulation principle: you need enough anchor structure to locate something, but not so much that you've already described the destination.
high.use: Consider whether your system's anchor intake should explicitly hold user inputs in a fuzzy state longer — resisting the impulse to resolve or sharpen what the user gives you until the matching step is complete, treating a half-articulated concept as a valid, high-surface input rather than an underspecified one that needs clarification first.
high.limit: FL-GLH is a closed, pre-specified system: every learning path, skill level, and diagnostic question is designed in advance by a domain expert, and nothing unexpected can surface from within it. Your system's wandering is the opposite — generative, domain-unconstrained, and prospectively unpredictable, with coherence emerging retrospectively from the path taken. The incompleteness FL-GLH preserves is quantitative fuzziness within a fixed schema; it has no mechanism for a half-formed vision to go looking for its own shape across unknown territory. The bridge holds for the 'vagueness as signal' and 'soft scoring without directing' shapes, but breaks entirely at the question of how unexpected resonance gets found in the first place.

high.spark: A reinforcement learning system built to discover new mathematics invented a scoring function that recognizes valuable territory without ever specifying a destination — and that mechanism is structurally identical to how your anchor is supposed to work.
high.source_shape: FERMAT treats mathematical theory formation as a Markov Decision Process: an agent moves through a vast space of possible mathematical objects, and at each step it scores what it finds using a learned 'interestingness' function I(m, S) — a heuristic that reads the current discovered set S and asks whether the new object m is worth keeping, without ever being told where to go next. The reward signal is sparse, meaning the agent receives almost no feedback during traversal and only occasionally encounters something that scores high. Over time, high-scoring subroutines get extracted and reused as scaffolding through a mechanism called EvoAbstract, compounding earlier finds into reusable structure rather than starting from scratch each time.
high.bridge: Your anchor plays the same role as FERMAT's interestingness function: it is a context that scores encountered material without directing the path. The agent doesn't ask 'is this the destination?' — it asks 'does this resonate with what I'm already carrying?' — which is exactly how soft anchoring is supposed to work. The MDP's sparse reward structure maps onto the past-trackable/future-unpredictable asymmetry: the only coherent account of where the agent has been is the retrospective trajectory, and no forward map exists before the traversal happens. And FERMAT's incompleteness is load-bearing in the same way yours is — a fully specified target would collapse the search into retrieval, not discovery; the partial skeleton is what keeps the match surface large enough to catch something surprising.
high.use: Consider whether FERMAT's interestingness function — score against current-state context, not against a fixed target — gives you a concrete operational template for implementing the soft anchor's scoring behavior: not 'does this match the anchor's content?' but 'does this shift the anchor's state in a way that feels generative?'
high.limit: FERMAT's interestingness function is trained against a ground-truth set of known mathematical entities — the reward fires when the agent rediscovers something already validated by human mathematicians. That means FERMAT is finding an open path toward a known answer. Your system requires an open path toward an unknown answer, where the anchor is a partial skeleton with no fixed destination and no pre-validated target set. If you tried to score resonance against a fixed reference the way FERMAT does, you would destroy the vagueness that makes the anchor useful — the system would converge on what is already known rather than surface what the user hasn't seen yet. The bridge holds for the scoring-without-directing shape; it breaks completely at the question of what the score is actually measured against.

The constraint is not an obstacle to understanding — it is the pressure that forces the agent to find the measurements that actually matter.
high.bridge: Your anchor is the measurement budget. It does not tell the wandering agents where to go — it tells them how much inferential weight each candidate connection must carry before it earns relevance. high.use: Consider auditing your anchor-scoring function through this lens: does each score reflect how much inferential leverage a connection provides relative to the current anchor, or does it reflect surface similarity? high.limit: MaD Physics operates in a closed universe where the law is real, fixed, and verifiable — the agent is recovering something that already exists and can be checked against ground truth. Constellax has no such ground truth: the user's vision is not a hidden law waiting to be measured, it is genuinely undetermined and shaped by the wandering itself. This means the benchmark's success criterion — correct prediction of future system state — has no analog here. You are not converging toward a correct answer; you are participating in constructing one. medium.source_shape: Fermat builds a reinforcement learning environment where an LLM-based evolutionary algorithm explores mathematical concept space and learns, through search, what makes a mathematical object interesting enough to develop further. medium.bridge: Your anchor works the same way at two key points. medium.spark: Mathematical creativity researchers discovered that two ideas can only truly fuse when both are *incomplete* — full, rigid structures don't blend, they collide. Your system's reliance on partial, underspecified anchors isn't a weakness in the design; it's the exact condition that makes generative connection possible.
medium.source_shape: Conceptual blending theory holds that new ideas emerge when two partial 'input spaces' — neither fully specified nor fully compatible with each other — are mapped onto a shared abstract skeleton called the 'generic space.' The generic space doesn't tell you what the blend will look like; it only tells you that the two inputs are compatible enough to be fused. The emergent blend is genuinely new: it isn't contained in either input, and it couldn't have been predicted from either alone. Crucially, the theory shows that completeness is the enemy of blending — the more rigidly defined an input space is, the less surface it offers for a productive fusion.
medium.bridge: Your anchor is the first input space, and it works precisely because it is underspecified — a skeleton, not a finished structure. The wandering agent's job is to find a second input space that shares enough abstract structure with the anchor to be blend-compatible, which is exactly what the generic space detects in formal blending theory. The agent's 'soft anchor scores without directing' mechanism maps directly onto the generic space's role: it doesn't dictate what the match must look like, it only registers when a structural kinship is present. And the anchor's principle that incompleteness is maximum match surface is not a metaphor here — blending theory formalizes the same claim: a fully articulated anchor would reduce the match surface to near-zero, because rigid structures can only collide with other rigid structures, not fuse with them.
medium.use: Consider framing the anchor's incompleteness not as a provisional state to be resolved, but as a permanent design parameter — the system might actively resist over-specification of the anchor, treating any attempt to 'complete' it as a signal that the match surface is shrinking. You could even surface this to users: when their anchor feels too vague to work with, that vagueness may be exactly what makes the system's connections land.
medium.limit: Blending theory is a dyadic operation: it requires two named input spaces that are both present before the blend can be analyzed. The formalism models the fusion event itself with precision, but it has nothing to say about how you find the second input space in the first place — the search phase, the wander, the traversal that ends when something blend-compatible is discovered, is entirely outside its scope. This means blending theory can validate and describe what happens when your agent finds a match, but it cannot model or guide the wandering that precedes the match. The bridge holds for the 'why incompleteness works' and 'what a soft anchor is doing' questions; it breaks the moment you ask it to explain how the agent should move through the space before it arrives anywhere.

medium.bridge: Your system's wandering agents operate on a soft anchor — a partially-specified skeleton of the user's concept — rather than a precise query. That asymmetry is deep: the study's logic is fully deterministic and backward-looking, measuring static states against a known target, while your anchor's power comes precisely from operating in territory where no target is known in advance. The wandering mechanism — dynamic traversal across unpredictable space — has no analog in the study at all. The structural match holds at the level of 'incompleteness enables transfer,' but breaks entirely at the question of how that incompleteness is used and whether it is ever meant to resolve.

The cluster also contains the strongest internal tension: some cards (P01-001, P01-002, P03-002) come from systems where incompleteness is eventually resolved into a correct answer, while the core claim of this system is that the incompleteness must be permanent.

clusters.label: Relational skeleton as recognition surface — topology over content
clusters.summary: Three cards from formal blending theory and one from theory blending AI (Sd86e36-001) converge on a more specific claim than the incompleteness cluster: it is not merely that the anchor is incomplete, but that what it preserves is relational topology — how the user's concepts connect to each other — rather than topical content. The generalization space (Sd86e36-001) and the generic space (P02-001, P02-002, S0b2583-001) are both minimal relational skeletons that enable recognition of structural kinship across surface-different domains. This cluster raises a concrete design question: is the anchor currently storing relational structure, or only topical content?

clusters.label: Acting under sparse signal — partial evidence as structural skeleton for inference
clusters.summary: MaD Physics (P06-001, P06-002, S43d63a-001) and FERMAT's MDP framing (P04-002) converge on a different angle: the wandering agent faces a budget problem structurally identical to a physics agent that must infer a law from a handful of measurements. Partial traversal is not a degraded version of full traversal — it is the condition that forces the agent to find structurally leveraged connections rather than exhaustive ones. The altered-laws design in MaD Physics maps onto the inheritance-vs-transcendence tension: the agent cannot retrieve a memorized answer because the ground has shifted, which is exactly the condition a wandering agent must maintain. These cards weakly suggest a concrete design test: does the scoring function reward triangulating leverage, or surface similarity?

clusters.label: Partial state as diagnostic address — locating the user in a possibility space
clusters.summary: Gagné's inheritance coding (P01-001), construct maps (P01-002), FL-GLH fuzzy assessment (P03-002), and fuzzy logic assessment (S195150-001) all share a structural move: a partial state — incomplete knowledge, vague input, unresolved competency — is treated as a precise locator in a possibility space rather than noise to be resolved before the system can act. The anchor-as-partial-state is a recognition signature, not a placeholder. These cards hold together less tightly than the blending cluster because the source systems are all convergent (they aim toward a correct answer), but the mechanism — partial encoding as diagnostic surface — transfers cleanly to the recognition function even if the destination logic does not.

clusters.label: Skeleton extraction from incomplete artifacts — honoring structure while transcending it
clusters.summary: Augmented Physics (P05-001, P05-002) offers a weaker but distinct angle: a system that animates static diagrams by extracting only a partial structural skeleton, without requiring a complete formal specification. The inheritance-vs-transcendence tension appears explicitly here — the system must honor the original artifact while producing something the original never contained. Both cards carry medium confidence and the bridge breaks at the question of whether vagueness is an obstacle or a mechanism, but the 'extract the scaffold, leave the rest open' engineering decision may be a concrete model for how the anchor intake step could be designed.

contradictions.description: Incompleteness as permanent vs. incompleteness as temporary: most source systems (Gagné, construct maps, FL-GLH, MaD Physics, Augmented Physics) treat partial states as deficits to be resolved toward a correct answer, while the system's core claim is that incompleteness must be permanent and load-bearing — resolving it destroys the function.

Scoring function as static vs. adaptive: FERMAT cards (P04-001, P04-002, S435cce-001, S413861-001, Sb4b50f-003) suggest the interestingness function should sharpen through the wander rather than be fixed at session start, while the construct-map and Gagné cards (P01-002, P03-002) imply the skeleton must be stable before any agent traversal happens — 'the map precedes the measurement.'

Anchor as pre-specified vs. anchor as emergent: blending theory (P02-001, P02-002, Sd86e36-001) requires the generic/generalization space to be identified before the blend runs, while FERMAT and MaD Physics suggest the scoring surface sharpens through traversal — the two models of when the anchor becomes usable are in tension.

Ground truth closure vs. open-ended discovery: FERMAT (P04-002) and MaD Physics (P06-001, P06-002, S43d63a-001) both ultimately score against a fixed reference set or a verifiable law — success is checkable — while the system requires scoring against a user's vague, pre-articulate vision with no ground truth, which breaks the reward signal logic both source systems depend on.

opportunity_paths.description: Design the anchor as a relational topology rather than a topical description — store how the user's concepts connect to each other, not just what they are, so the scoring function can detect structural kinship across surface-different domains.

open_questions: When should the anchor be stable vs. adaptive — does it need to be fixed before wandering begins (so agents have something to triangulate against), or should it sharpen through the session (so early finds inform later scoring)? Several cards point in opposite directions on this.

What is the anchor actually storing — topical content, relational topology, or both? The blending theory cards suggest relational topology is the load-bearing part, but the current design may not distinguish between the two.

Is there an 'over-specification brake' in the system — a signal that fires when the anchor or the agent's path is converging too tightly on the literal terms of the input too early, before surprising structural resonances have had a chance to surface?

How should partial matches be presented to the user — as a single confidence scalar, or as a structured map of which dimensions matched and which didn't? The fuzzy logic and blending cards suggest the gap pattern is as informative as the hit pattern.

If the scoring function has no ground truth to close the loop on, what prevents it from drifting toward rewarding familiarity rather than structural novelty? FERMAT and MaD Physics both depend on verifiable feedback; this system cannot.

Is the incompleteness of the anchor meant to be permanent by design, or does it resolve over the course of a session as the user engages with cards? The answer to this question changes the architecture substantially.
Can the 'weakening pass' idea — adjusting the anchor's framing minimally to make a tension-producing find coherent — be operationalized without collapsing the anchor's match surface in the process?

What degree of anchor incompleteness maximizes resonant finds without losing scoring coherence — is there a tunable parameter here, and if so, what signal would indicate it is set too high or too low?
synthesis.recommended_next_direction: The session's cards most strongly converge around two related design questions the user might explore together: what the anchor is actually storing (relational topology vs. topical content), and whether the scoring function should be static at session start or adaptive through the wander. The blending theory cluster — particularly Sd86e36-001 and P02-002 — suggests that relational topology is the load-bearing part of the anchor, and that the session's card-response loop might already be functioning as a blending iteration without being explicitly designed as one. If the user examined the anchor's current representation against the 'generalization space' concept — asking whether it captures how the user's concepts connect to each other, not just what they are — that audit might surface a concrete architectural decision about what the intake step should extract and preserve. The FERMAT cluster raises a different but adjacent question: whether the scoring function that recognizes resonance could be designed to sharpen through the session rather than being fixed at the start, which would change how early, vague anchors are handled and might resolve the tension between 'the map precedes the measurement' and 'the scorer emerges from the search.'
synthesis.what_would_change_the_verdict: If evidence emerged that users whose anchors were sharpened and fully articulated before wandering began produced better outcomes than users whose anchors remained vague throughout the session, the core claim that incompleteness is the mechanism — not a temporary deficiency — would need to be revised, and the design logic of the entire intake and scoring layer would shift.

card.spark: Mathematical invention works by merging two incomplete theories — and it is precisely their incompleteness that makes the merge productive. Your system is built on the same principle: the half-formed concept is not a problem to fix before the work begins, it is the mechanism by which the work begins.
card.bridge: The anchor's soft-anchor mechanism and Bou et al.'s generic space are the same structural object: something rich enough to recognize that two things belong together, but empty enough not to predetermine what emerges from that recognition. In both cases, the anchor/generic-space does not say 'go here' — it says 'these two things are in structural conversation, now run the blend and see what falls out.' The paper's operationalization of incompleteness-as-match-surface maps directly onto the anchor's treatment of the user's half-formed concept: a fully articulated, closed concept would be like a fully axiomatized theory — nothing new can emerge because there is no open surface for incoming structure to attach to. The user's vagueness is not noise to be cleaned before the system runs; it is the exact feature that makes resonance possible. Where the paper uses iterative inconsistency-resolution to crystallize the blend into a stable new theory, your system's process of surfacing connections and letting the user respond is doing the same work — the card the user engages with is the blend being 'run,' and the user's reaction is the inconsistency-resolution step that tightens or weakens the emerging structure.
card.source_shape: Bou et al. model mathematical discovery as conceptual blending: two partial theories — say, a fragment of natural number arithmetic and a fragment of functional analysis — are fed into a formal process that finds their shared skeleton (the 'generic space') and constructs a new theory, called the colimit, that inherits structure from both without being identical to either. The key move is that neither input theory is complete. Full, closed theories have no room left for emergent structure — every consequence is already derivable. It is the gaps, the missing axioms, the unresolved tensions between the two inputs, that the blending process fills with something genuinely new. The system then 'runs' the blend: it derives theorems that were not present in either input, discovers inconsistencies, weakens axioms to resolve them, and iterates.
card.use: Consider framing the user's concept not as an input to be clarified before the system works, but as a deliberately partial axiomatization — and design the card-response loop explicitly as a blending iteration: each card the user engages with adds a constraint that tightens the generic space, and each card they reject weakens an implicit axiom, until the colimit (their advanced concept) crystallizes from the accumulated resolutions.
card.limit: The blending process in Bou et al. is fully deterministic and formally verifiable: given the two input spaces and the morphisms connecting them to the generic space, the colimit is uniquely computed and its consistency is checkable by machine. That requires the human to name both input theories and identify the generic space before the machinery runs — the entire structure is drawn in advance, then executed. Your system's premise is the opposite: the user cannot and should not name those spaces, because the wandering agents are what surface them. There is also no counterpart in the paper to the retrospective-path-as-coherent-map: in conceptual blending, the diagram precedes the blend; in your system, the only coherent account of why a path was productive emerges after the path is walked. The bridge holds tightly for the incompleteness-as-productive-surface and generic-space-as-soft-anchor shapes, but breaks entirely at the question of who specifies the morphisms and when.

known.prior_work_name: Conceptual Blending via colimit (Bou, Schorlemmer et al.)
known.reference: Bou, Corneli, Schorlemmer et al., 'The Role of Blending in Mathematical Invention', ICCC 2015
known.reasoning: The bridge maps the anchor to the generic-space/colimit construction of formal conceptual blending, which is the documented framework the card explicitly describes.

card.spark: A branch of mathematics built to explain how new concepts are born treats incompleteness not as a flaw to fix but as the very surface that makes merging possible — which is exactly the structural bet your system is making.
card.bridge: Your system's anchor plays the same role as the generic space in blending theory: it is not a target the agent must reach, it is a relational skeleton that tells the agent which finds are structurally compatible with the user's concept. The colimit's 'score without directing' property is the formal version of your soft anchor — it provides just enough constraint to recognize a coherent merge without predetermining what gets merged. The paper's treatment of incompleteness as generative maps directly onto your system's assumption that a vague, unfinished anchor is an asset rather than a liability: the less the anchor specifies, the more structural surface it exposes for an unexpected find to attach to. And the weakening move — negotiating inconsistency rather than rejecting it — suggests that when an agent surfaces something that only partially fits the anchor, the right response is not discard but partial inheritance, letting the user's concept absorb what fits and leave the rest.
card.source_shape: Conceptual Blending theory, formalized here using a construction called a colimit, describes how a genuinely new concept emerges when two partial structures from different domains are merged into a blended space that inherits properties from both inputs but cannot be reduced to either one alone. The colimit acts as a minimal common scaffold: it does not dictate what the blend will look like, it only identifies the structural relationships that must be preserved for the merge to be coherent. Critically, when two input spaces are inconsistent with each other, the formalism does not discard the blend — it weakens the conflicting constraints just enough to restore consistency, treating the tension as something to negotiate rather than a reason to stop.
card.use: Consider treating partial or tension-producing matches not as low-confidence noise to filter out, but as candidates for a weakening pass — a step that asks 'what is the minimal adjustment to the anchor's framing that would make this find coherent?' and surfaces that adjusted framing to the user alongside the find itself.
card.limit: The formalism here is entirely human-driven: a mathematician consciously selects both input spaces and the generic space that mediates them before the colimit is ever computed. The theory is a precise account of how to blend once you already know what to blend — it has no mechanism for autonomous traversal, and no way to score an unknown find against a vague anchor before the blend is even attempted. Your agents must solve the upstream problem the paper assumes away: discovering candidate input spaces in the first place, without a human pre-selecting them. The bridge holds for the blending and incompleteness-handling shape; it breaks entirely at the question of how to wander.

known.prior_work_name: Conceptual Blending Theory (Fauconnier & Turner) with colimit formalization
known.reference: Fauconnier & Turner 2002, 'The Way We Think'; Goguen & Harrell colimit formalization
known.reasoning: The bridge transfers the generic-space-as-soft-scaffold property of formal conceptual blending, a documented family of work.

card.spark: A branch of AI formalized how to build genuinely new concepts by finding the structural skeleton two theories share — not retrieving what's similar, but synthesizing what neither contained alone. That skeleton logic is the same mechanism your anchor uses to recognize resonance without predetermining it.
card.bridge: Your anchor plays the role of G. When a user gives Constellax their half-formed pursuit and unresolved inspirations, that input is not a query to be matched against a database — it is a structural skeleton that defines what counts as resonant without specifying what the resonant thing will look like. The wandering agent's job is anti-unification in reverse: traverse a domain, find structures that share relational topology with the anchor, and surface them as candidate blends. The paper's scoring logic — does this candidate preserve the skeleton's topology while adding something neither input contained? — is exactly the test a well-functioning Constellax card should pass. And the paper's central finding, that the generalization space enables synthesis rather than retrieval, names why the anchor must stay loose: a tighter anchor would retrieve known things; a skeletal anchor creates the conditions for something new to emerge.
card.source_shape: The paper formalizes 'theory blending': given two input theories L and R, an algorithm finds their least-general common structure — called the generalization space G — using a technique called anti-unification. G is not a summary of either theory; it is the minimal relational skeleton that both theories satisfy. Candidate blends are then scored by how well they preserve the topology of G while introducing new content neither L nor R contained. The key move is that G acts as a recognition surface — rich enough to identify when a blend is structurally coherent, but loose enough to leave the actual content of the blend undetermined.
card.use: Consider auditing your anchor's representation against the paper's generalization space concept — specifically, ask whether the anchor is currently storing enough relational structure (how the user's concepts connect to each other) rather than just topical content, since it is relational topology, not subject matter, that enables cross-domain resonance of the kind this paper formalizes.
card.limit: This formalization requires both input theories to be fully axiomatized in many-sorted first-order logic before the algorithm can run — the inputs must be logically coherent and complete. Constellax's anchor is the opposite: deliberately pre-axiomatic, structurally incomplete, and valuable precisely because of that incompleteness. The paper's framework would treat the anchor's vagueness as a preprocessing deficiency to resolve before blending can begin; Constellax treats that same vagueness as the maximum match surface — the thing that keeps the recognition space open. The two systems are structurally analogous up to that point, and diverge exactly where the user's incompleteness matters most.

known.prior_work_name: Theory blending via anti-unification / generalization space
known.reference: Schmidt, Krumnack, Gust, Kühnberger, 'Heuristic-Driven Theory Projection', and Martinez et al. blending via anti-unification
known.reasoning: The bridge matches the documented anti-unification generalization-space mechanism for computational theory blending.

card.spark: Mathematical creativity researchers discovered that two ideas can only truly fuse when both are *incomplete* — full, rigid structures don't blend, they collide. Your system's reliance on partial, underspecified anchors isn't a weakness in the design; it's the exact condition that makes generative connection possible.
card.bridge: Your anchor is the first input space, and it works precisely because it is underspecified — a skeleton, not a finished structure. The wandering agent's job is to find a second input space that shares enough abstract structure with the anchor to be blend-compatible, which is exactly what the generic space detects in formal blending theory. The agent's 'soft anchor scores without directing' mechanism maps directly onto the generic space's role: it doesn't dictate what the match must look like, it only registers when a structural kinship is present. And the anchor's principle that incompleteness is maximum match surface is not a metaphor here — blending theory formalizes the same claim: a fully articulated anchor would reduce the match surface to near-zero, because rigid structures can only collide with other rigid structures, not fuse with them.
card.source_shape: Conceptual blending theory holds that new ideas emerge when two partial 'input spaces' — neither fully specified nor fully compatible with each other — are mapped onto a shared abstract skeleton called the 'generic space.' The generic space doesn't tell you what the blend will look like; it only tells you that the two inputs are compatible enough to be fused. The emergent blend is genuinely new: it isn't contained in either input, and it couldn't have been predicted from either alone. Crucially, the theory shows that completeness is the enemy of blending — the more rigidly defined an input space is, the less surface it offers for a productive fusion.
card.use: Consider framing the anchor's incompleteness not as a provisional state to be resolved, but as a permanent design parameter — the system might actively resist over-specification of the anchor, treating any attempt to 'complete' it as a signal that the match surface is shrinking. You could even surface this to users: when their anchor feels too vague to work with, that vagueness may be exactly what makes the system's connections land.
card.limit: Blending theory is a dyadic operation: it requires two named input spaces that are both present before the blend can be analyzed. The formalism models the fusion event itself with precision, but it has nothing to say about how you find the second input space in the first place — the search phase, the wander, the traversal that ends when something blend-compatible is discovered, is entirely outside its scope. This means blending theory can validate and describe what happens when your agent finds a match, but it cannot model or guide the wandering that precedes the match. The bridge holds for the 'why incompleteness works' and 'what a soft anchor is doing' questions; it breaks the moment you ask it to explain how the agent should move through the space before it arrives anywhere.

known.reasoning: The bridge transfers the generic-space compatibility-detection role of conceptual blending, a documented framework.

card.spark: A reinforcement learning system for automated mathematics solved the exact problem your anchor mechanism is trying to solve — how to guide a search without predetermining where it lands — by splitting the scoring layer completely away from the search path.
card.bridge: Your anchor plays the same structural role as FERMAT's interestingness function: it does not tell the wandering agents where to go, it recognizes whether what they found is worth surfacing. The split FERMAT makes explicit — search mechanism on one side, scoring mechanism on the other — is the same split your architecture depends on when it lets agents wander freely and then measures resonance against the anchor after the fact. There is a second correspondence: FERMAT's interestingness function starts incomplete and sharpens through contact with the search results, which mirrors the way a user's anchor is a partial skeleton that gains definition through what it successfully recognizes. The anchor does not need to be fully articulated in advance; like FERMAT's scoring function, it becomes more itself through the process of matching.
card.source_shape: FERMAT is an RL environment that forms mathematical theories by evolving an 'interestingness' function: a learned measure that scores candidate outputs as worth keeping or not, without specifying which outputs to generate. The search roams combinatorially vast spaces of mathematical objects freely; the interestingness function only fires after a candidate surfaces, judging it without having directed the path to it. Critically, the interestingness function itself is never fully defined in advance — it is synthesized and sharpened through the process, gaining definition from what it encounters.
card.use: Consider examining how FERMAT's LLM-based evolutionary algorithm handles abstraction across structurally similar but surface-different mathematical objects — that mechanism is the closest published analog to cross-domain resonance scoring, and studying how it avoids requiring symbolic grounding might surface a concrete design move for your own scoring layer.
card.limit: FERMAT operates entirely inside a single formally bounded domain — elementary number theory and finite fields — where every object has a crisp symbolic definition and every claim is verifiably true or false. That formal verifiability is what makes the RL reward signal tractable. Constellax's wandering is explicitly cross-domain and metaphorical: whether a theater concept resonates with a physics paper is irreducibly interpretive, not computable. The bridge holds for the structural shape — scoring without directing, incompleteness that sharpens through use — but breaks at the question of how to operationalize that scoring function when there is no formal ground truth to close the loop on.

card.spark: A reinforcement learning system for automated mathematics had to solve the exact same tension your system does: deterministic search kills surprise, pure randomness kills usefulness — so it learned a soft scoring function that guides exploration without ever specifying the destination.
card.bridge: Your anchor plays the role of the interestingness function. It does not tell the wandering agents where to go — it scores what they bring back, pulling toward resonance without predetermining the path. The wandering agents' traversal maps onto Fermat's MDP: each hop is a local move made without long-range memory of the full trajectory, and the only coherent map of where the agent went is the retrospective path it leaves behind. The structural match goes one level deeper: Fermat's core causal claim — that determinism collapses novelty and pure randomness collapses utility — is the same causal primitive your mechanism layer names as the reason a soft anchor is necessary at all. The IF is not just analogous to your anchor; it is an implemented, tested instance of the same mechanism operating in a different substrate.
card.source_shape: Fermat frames mathematical theory formation as a Markov Decision Process: an agent traverses a space of mathematical concepts and operations, building new theorems step by step, with no long-range memory of where it has been. The problem it had to solve is that neither a fixed search strategy nor random mutation produces interesting mathematics — so the system learns an 'interestingness function' (IF) through an evolutionary algorithm, a scoring mechanism that recognizes when a newly formed concept is worth pursuing without ever being told in advance what 'worth pursuing' means. The IF is the middle path: rich enough to recognize signal when the wander encounters it, loose enough to permit traversal into regions that were never named before training.
card.use: Consider examining Fermat's evolutionary algorithm for the IF as a concrete existence proof that this kind of scoring function can be learned rather than hand-specified — if your anchor's scoring logic ever needs to be made more explicit or adaptive, the IF architecture is a worked example of how that problem was solved in a formally tractable domain.
card.limit: Fermat's interestingness function is trained and evaluated entirely within a single formally bounded domain — elementary number theory and Galois fields. It learns what is interesting inside mathematics, and it would need to be rebuilt from scratch for any other domain. Your system's wandering is explicitly cross-domain and unconstrained: the anchor must recognize structural resonance across theater, physics, literature, and mathematics simultaneously, using the same scoring logic. The IF has no mechanism for cross-domain structural transfer, which is precisely the three-point triangulation your architecture depends on — so the bridge holds tightly for the freedom-within-constraint shape and the soft-scoring mechanism, but breaks completely at the question of how a single anchor function generalizes across domains it was never trained on.

card.spark: A benchmark that forces AI agents to infer physical laws from a tight measurement budget — and breaks agents that rely on memorized knowledge — turns out to be a working model of how productive incompleteness actually functions: partial signal isn't noise to be resolved, it's the structural skeleton that guides the search.
card.bridge: Your system's wandering agents face the same three-way tension: exhaustive retrieval collapses novelty (the agent finds what is already known), pure drift collapses utility (the agent finds what is merely distant), and the anchor is what makes the middle path possible. In MaD Physics, the measurement budget is the analog of your agents' finite traversal — it forces the agent to act on partial information rather than wait for certainty. The altered physical law is the analog of your user's anchor: a structural shape that is real and recoverable, but not yet explicit, and not matchable by memorized pattern. And the agent's behavior of treating partial observations as a skeleton — not noise, not a complete picture, but a genuine partial structure that constrains the next move — is exactly what your 'incompleteness as maximum match surface' mechanism claims: the anchor's vagueness is not a deficiency to be resolved before the search begins, it is what keeps the search space open enough to find resonance the user didn't already know to name.
card.source_shape: MaD Physics presents agents with a physical system governed by a law that may have been altered — spring constants shifted, pendulum parameters changed — and gives them a limited number of observations before demanding a prediction. Agents that try to exhaustively measure everything run out of budget before they can act; agents that move randomly waste measurements on uninformative states. The agents that succeed are the ones that treat each partial observation as a structural clue — something that narrows the space of possible laws without yet resolving it — and use that narrowing to decide where to look next. Crucially, when the underlying law is altered, agents trained on the standard version fail badly, because their memorized priors override the signal the environment is actually emitting.
card.use: Consider testing whether your agents perform differently when they are explicitly rewarded for returning findings that partially match the anchor rather than findings that fully satisfy it — MaD Physics suggests that the agents trained to act on incomplete structural signal outperform those waiting for resolution, and you could design a scoring pass that privileges 'structurally adjacent but not obvious' returns over high-confidence direct matches.
card.limit: MaD Physics operates inside a single, closed environment per trial — one physical system, one law, a known structural form even when its parameters are altered. Your agents wander across unconstrained domains simultaneously: theater, mathematics, biology, whatever the traversal reaches. The benchmark's 'altered law' is still a law — it has a recoverable symbolic form, and the agent's job is parameter inference inside a known family. Your anchor is user-generated and deliberately vague, with no recoverable symbolic form and no guarantee that the underlying structure belongs to any named family at all. The bridge holds for the shape of acting-on-partial-signal, but breaks at the question of how an agent knows when it has found a genuine structural match versus a superficial one — MaD Physics has ground truth; your system does not.

card.spark: An AI that must infer the laws of physics from a handful of expensive measurements — never allowed to just observe everything — is solving the same structural problem as a system that must find what's most relevant to a concept without being told what to look for.
card.bridge: Your system's anchor works the same way a MaD Physics budget works: it doesn't tell the wandering agent where to go, it just makes some directions more expensive than others by scoring resonance softly rather than directing the path. The agent's partial traversal of a domain — never complete, always constrained — is structurally identical to the MaD Physics agent's sparse measurement set: both are incomplete by design, and that incompleteness is what keeps the possibility space open long enough to find something genuinely surprising. The 'altered laws' design choice maps onto your inheritance-vs-transcendence tension: MaD Physics deliberately breaks known physics so the agent cannot retrieve a memorized answer, which is exactly the condition your wandering agents need to satisfy — the find must be a genuine structural resonance, not a cached association. And just as a MaD Physics agent that over-commits to early measurements collapses the inference space prematurely, a wandering agent that locks onto the anchor's most obvious surface meaning will stop finding the non-obvious bridges.
card.source_shape: MaD Physics is a benchmark where an agent studies a physical system operating under deliberately altered laws — laws the agent cannot have memorized — and must figure out those laws using only a strict budget of measurements. Each measurement costs something, so the agent cannot simply observe until certainty arrives; it must choose which observations will unlock the most inference from the least data. The design forces the agent to work from a partial skeleton of evidence and reason forward, rather than pattern-match against prior knowledge.
card.use: Consider whether your wandering agents have an explicit 'over-specification brake' — a signal that fires when the agent's path is converging too tightly on the anchor's literal terms too early, analogous to the MaD Physics budget that forces the agent to stay in the inferential space longer before committing.
card.limit: MaD Physics is a convergent system with a finish line: the agent's incompleteness is a temporary constraint it is actively trying to eliminate, and success means closing the gap to a single correct answer. Your system treats vagueness as a permanent structural feature to be preserved, not a problem to be solved — the anchor is never meant to be fully resolved into a definitive match. MaD Physics also operates entirely within a single domain with a known target structure; your wandering agents cross unconstrained domain space looking for structural resonance in theater, biology, or wherever the wander leads, and MaD Physics has no mechanism that corresponds to that cross-domain traversal at all.

card.spark: A mathematics assessment system deliberately refuses to resolve vagueness — it keeps '59' and '60' in the same fuzzy category on purpose, because the blur is the signal. Your system's productive incompleteness works the same way: the half-formed concept isn't a problem to fix before processing begins, it's the input that makes wide matching possible.
card.bridge: Your anchor mechanism — a soft score that guides without directing — is exactly what the fuzzy membership function implements: a 59 and a 60 are not categorically different, so the system doesn't force a hard boundary that would eliminate valid matches near the edge. This maps directly to your claim that full articulation narrows the match surface to near-zero: the moment you resolve vagueness prematurely, you lose the productive overlap zone where resonance lives. The Gagné hierarchy maps to the structural skeleton node — it is a minimum viable diagnostic scaffold, not a complete map, and partial knowledge at one level is treated as a real signal about barrier location rather than noise. The four grouped inputs FL-GLH uses as its diagnostic frame mirror your three-point triangulation principle: you need enough anchor structure to locate something, but not so much that you've already described the destination.
card.source_shape: Fuzzy Logic with a Gagné Learning Hierarchy (FL-GLH) takes imprecise linguistic inputs — 'Poor,' 'Developing,' 'Good' — and runs them through a fuzzifier that preserves the vagueness structurally rather than converting it to a precise number. Underneath this sits a Gagné hierarchy: a prerequisite skill chain where each level depends on the one below it, so a gap at any point is a precise locator of where learning broke down. The system only collapses vagueness into a crisp output at the very last step — defuzzification — meaning the entire diagnostic process operates on soft, overlapping categories rather than sharp distinctions.
card.use: Consider whether your system's anchor intake should explicitly hold user inputs in a fuzzy state longer — resisting the impulse to resolve or sharpen what the user gives you until the matching step is complete, treating a half-articulated concept as a valid, high-surface input rather than an underspecified one that needs clarification first.
card.limit: FL-GLH is a closed, pre-specified system: every learning path, skill level, and diagnostic question is designed in advance by a domain expert, and nothing unexpected can surface from within it. Your system's wandering is the opposite — generative, domain-unconstrained, and prospectively unpredictable, with coherence emerging retrospectively from the path taken.
